Title: Summer Ramble in South Warrington 2026
Summary:
Join Andrew for a summer walk in south Warrington.
We will start by visiting Walton Hall and Gardens and then walk up to the Walton reservoir and make our way to Stretton via tracks, round the side of fields and along the route of an old Roman Road (see if we can spot the statues of Roman soldiers).
When we get to Stretton we will stop for a welcome drink at the Stretton Fox. It might be possible to eat our butties here or you could buy food in the pub if you haven't bought any butties.
We will then set off down Hatton lane, across fields and down country lanes until we get to Hill Cliffe where we can enjoy a panoramic view of Warrington. We will then make our way back to the start and could then call at the Walton Arms for a summer drink or coffee and cakes in the cafe at Walton gardens.
The nearest railway stations are Warrington Central and Warrington Bank Quay. If you're coming by train let Andrew know and it should be possible for someone to meet you at the station and give you a lift to the start of the walk.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event
?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don't worry if the event is full, put your name on the waiting list and you will get a place if someone cancels before the event.
All photos taken by Andrew
Difficulty & Distance: Mainly country lanes and tracks; some road walking;  about 8 miles.

Title: Open water swim (Yorkshire)
Summary:
If you enjoy open water swimming or have been thinking about giving it a go, then come with OutdoorLads to take the plunge!
You will need to be a confident and strong swimmer.
This event would not suit new or less confident swimmers.
There are 2 set routes, 250 metres and 500 metres.
We can swim between 8am - 10am. We will meet at about 8am and then people can change and hire suits if needed. If we get in the water at 8.30am it still gives us plenty of time. The venue is an old quarry, so the water is still, clean and safe.
Safety: the venue is fully organised and staffed with lifeguards and medics to ensure the safety of swimmers.
Please read the following link if you intend to swim. The videos are very good and help explain how things work.
https://www.bluelagooners.com
Kit: you can hire gear at the venue if you don't own your own. Even on a very warm day the water will be cool, so it is advisable to swim in a full swim suit.
The facilities are decent. There are changing cubicles but no showers. There is also a cafe if you want a drink or bite to eat.
You will need to book a place through the
Bluelagooners website
. The swim costs £10.
Signing up to this event page does not guarantee you entry to the swim. You need to be booked onto both to be part of this swim with OutdoorLads.
The activity is not run by OutdoorLads but by Bluelagooners.The event is open to the public but there will be plenty of room for everyone 🙂.
This is a third part event - OutdoorLads is arranging a group to meet together there only. You will be subject to the standard terms, conditions and insurance of Bluelagooners and will need to sign any waiver they require to participate
.
Dogs are not allowed at this venue.
Image credits:  all photos provided by the event leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: You will need to be a strong swimmer.

Title: Tom a' Chòinich & Toll Creagach, Glen Affric
Summary:
A duo of Munros in arguably the most beautiful glen of all, Affric!
These are two very different but neighbouring Munros closer to the entrance (and thus the car parks) in Glen Affric, but nevertheless they are still a challenging day out.
Tom a' Chòinich has rocky ridges and an appealing peak, Toll Creagach is a more rounded, mossy summit. The former is 1112m, the latter 1054m.
Our route is 16.5km or 10.25miles. Some of the route involves boggy ground and crossing a couple of burns (if in spate, the walk will be altered). Total ascent 1096m.
Dogs are welcome but must be kept under close control.
Photos are taken by AMD and are used with his gracious permission.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our Telegram
chat
and
notification
groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: Remote, pathless in places, boggy in places, two streams to cross, 1096 m of ascent.

Title: See Loch Ness From Above
Summary:
Probably the most famous stretch of water in the world.
If you've not yet seen the beloved Nessie, maybe viewing her home from above will improve your chances!! If you don't know what to look for, shots of Nessie and her offspring are included in the photos on this page......
Loch Ness, as well as being home to Nessie, has a few other impressive facts about it (yes, Nessie's residence in the Loch is a fact!!) Loch Ness contains more water that all the rivers and lakes of England combined. It is far deeper than any part of the vast North Sea.
Enough marketing, let's get on with our day out.
We'll be seeing Loch Ness from above by climbing up a lovely hill called Meall Fuar-mhonaidh. That's easy for you to say. It stands proud at 699m above sea level.
The walk is out and back, 9km / 6miles and takes in some beautiful woodland before ascending slopes on open hillside. There are a few short, steep sections, but nothing of any real challenge. The walk is suitable for any reasonably active hill walker.
From the summit, if conditions allow, we should be able to see almost the full length of Loch Ness, with just a litre or two towards Inverness obscured by the hills. In the other direction, way beyond Loch Ness in the Great Glen, we should see Ben Nevis towering above Fort William. In other directions, we get a glimpse of seemingly endless summits in the Highlands. Finally, with a stroke of luck, we might see Nessie in her waters or sunning herself on a remote lochside beach.
Dogs are welcome on this walk, kept under close control.
Photos:
taken by Rob Mitchell and used with permission.

groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our

Title: Pentland Hills
Summary:
A joint walk with the
Gay Outdoor Club
in the Pentland Hills from Hillend.
An eight mile walk from the Pentland ski centre car park at Hillend, climbing Caerketton Hill, Allermuir Hill, with an option to also climb Capelaw Hill.  About eight miles, 4 - 5 hours walking . The start point is accessible by public transport from Edinburgh. This is a hill walk so expect some steep climbs and descents and paths may be muddy after rain.
There are many variations of the route we can take and we can cut the walk short or extend it if the weather is great. An idea of some of the route is on the
Walkhighlands website.
Photos :
taken by
david396
on a previous GOC walk in the Pentlands in 2009.
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

Title: Woburn Estate
Summary:
Join David and Alex for varied circular route through the Woburn Estate and surrounding countryside. We’ll start from the main car park opposite St Mary’s Church and head out through the Deer Park, skirting lakes and woodland and keeping an eye out for black squirrels and deer along the way.
We’ll follow a mix of public footpaths, estate tracks and quiet lanes, including sections alongside the safari park fences - so there’s a good chance of seeing wildlife, even if the lions stay put. The route leaves the Woburn Estate and takes us through villages like Eversholt and Milton Bryan, where there’s an optional pub stop near the halfway point.
Later on we’ll pass some interesting historical spots, including a former WWII propaganda site built in 1942 as a studio for the Political Warfare Executive, and Paris House (which a previous Duke of Bedford brought back from the Paris Exhibition of 1878), before looping back through the estate past ponds and open grassland to finish where we started.
It’s mostly gentle walking with a few steady rises, nothing technical, and we’ll keep a relaxed pace with stops as needed. A short section may involve paddle along a stream, which can be avoided if not everyone is wearing waterproof footwear!
There are warning signs in the Woburn estate as the large number of deer means there may be ticks present, so please be aware. The grass us usually grazed short so usually only a problem if picnicking!
Don't waste that space!
Summer means lots of members looking to get out more so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
The walk is not suitable for dogs as it crosses a deer park and the edge of a safari park.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse
Difficulty & Distance: Mostly gentle walking. Around 4 hours walking time.

Title: From Harbour to Shore - a Wittering Walk to Adore
Summary:
Only five people attended this walk when I ran it before, so perhaps I can get away with repeating it to plug a gap in the southeast walk schedules, and more guys will find it a novel experience. Starting in Chichester, we'll follow the calm, tree-lined Chichester Canal south until it opens into Chichester Harbour, sparkling, spacious and special. We'll stroll past Birdham’s marinas and along Itchenor’s winding lanes, with seabirds calling overhead and sails on the horizon. We'll end our journey on the golden sands of West Wittering. Fresh air, fine views, and a frolic on the sand on one of the best beaches around, and as this is a linear walk, a spell relaxing on the bus back.
The sights:
Birdham:
Some pleasant houses, especially the Westlands Estate. Birdham Pool was a tidal mill pond but became one of the first public marinas in the country in 1946. Chichester Marina is the chief attraction: a big rectangular harbour with sleek yachts along pontoons, created in 1966. St James' Church is big and bulky C14 but harshly restored in 1882.
Chichester Harbour AONB:
'Chichester Harbour is one of the few remaining undeveloped coastal areas in Southern England...Its wide expanses and intricate creeks are at the same time a major wildlife haven and among some of Britain's most popular boating waters. The massive stretch of tidal flats and saltings are of outstanding ecological significance.' (From Wikipedia)
West Itchenor:
'One short, nicely unpretentious street of cottages, mostly 18th century, running down to the water's edge.' (Ian Nairn,
The Buildings of England: Sussex
). Huge waterside properties all around. From 1744 to the early C19 warships where intermittently made there.
West Wittering:
An attractive and exclusive village with large thatched houses and villas set in large gardens. Cakeham Manor was once a palace of the bishops of Chichester, now an attractive group of medieval, Tudor and Georgian parts. The tall brick tower of the early C16 stands out amid the flat fields.
East Head
and West Wittering Beach:
'One of the last surviving pieces of natural coastline in West Sussex. East Head is an example of the nationally rare and fragile, yet dynamic sand-dune habitat. Walk, play and sunbathe on the beach, which is considered one of the best in Sussex [it has Blue Flag status (see the
website
)].' (From the National Trust website). The car park and beach get horribly busy in the summer, but our walk beforehand will be relatively quiet.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them. Dogs are excluded from the main swimming beach at West Wittering but are allowed nearby. There are dog waste bins. Warm weather could be exhausting for dogs although we are close to water most of the way. Any dog off its lead must be under control.
The
route
(please click on the link to see it)
:
Starting at Chichester Railway Station, we'll follow the New Lipchis Way all the way along the canal to the beach. After the beach, we'll walk to Cakeham Road to catch the bus back to Chichester.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance. The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby and the beach retains its Blue Flag award every year. Lifeguards are in attendance at the beach.

. The other pictures were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance: 18 km (11.5 miles); terrain: almost flat and on beach shingle, grass, dirt, gravel, sand and tarmac; total climb is just 14m.

Title: London Bouldering Morning - All Abilities welcome!
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
If you have not been to one of the London Climbing Centres you will need to pre-register
HERE
. You only need to register once to use any of their 8 centres in London.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
First time boulderer?
If you have never tried bouldering before, the event leader can give you a short introduction to get you started and give a few a few pointers on technique. After which we will join in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Experienced boulderer?
Simply register with the wall and turn up on the day!
As above, all attending the event must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible for your own safety and so should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Difficulty & Distance: With climbs at every grade from complete beginner to seasoned pro, you decide how hard it gets!

Title: Hereford and Breinton Springs
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow and largely flat 10km / 6 mile walk from Hereford Cathedral to the National Trust site of
Breinton Springs
.
This short walk starts and finishes in the cathedral grounds in Hereford and takes a leisurely stroll along the river Wye.
Along the way you get some stunning views of the cathedral and the river before ending up in a peaceful churchyard surrounded by ancient orchards and the possibility of coming face to face with some endangered wildlife in an SSSI. We also walk past the spot where a dog fell into the Wye more than a hundred years ago, inspiring Elgar to write one of the
Enigma Variations
. On our return to the city we take a loop through the site where
Hereford Castle
once stood.
The walk is planned for the end of September, so if you wished to stay in Hereford until the following day, you could join the
Queers & Beers monthly meet up
, which normally takes place on the last Sunday of the month. And if you were to arrive early, you could pop to the café in All Saints Church to check out a
particularly rude wood carving
.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

As part of Reading's commitment to helping the community get outside for their mental and physical well being we will have a stall in the Sports & Outdoor zone and this gives OutdoorLads the chance to engage with potential new members.  We are looking for 6 ODLer's to help cover the stall.
Reading is easily accessible so why not make a mini break of it and join us for a variety of events over the weekend!
Pride Events
Reading Pride is
free,
there will be a main stage and a number of different zones you are free to enjoy the rest of pride, part take in the planned sports events or just enjoy the day

Title: Midweek Meander - Iron Giant Meatballs
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow walk in Derbyshire, taking in the Iron Giant and ending up in the IKEA restaurant.
The Bennerley Viaduct, known informally as the Iron Giant, and occasionally described as
Derbyshire's Eiffel Tower
is a wrought iron structure that once carried a railway. It proved impossible to demolish, got listed in the 1970s and recently has been cared for by a friends group who have worked hard to get a decent footpath over the top of it.
IKEA
opened in Nottinghamshire in 1997
and revolutionised home furnishings in the East Midlands. Their low priced café is where we will finish our walk and while they are most famous for their meatballs, their menu includes veggie options. Signing up for an
IKEA Family card
in advance might give you a deal in the restaurant.
This walk includes a Nottinghamshire furniture giant and Derbyshire iron giant. The boundary between the two counties is the River Erewash which we will cross.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
Image Credits:
All images provided by the walk leader and permission is granted to ODL for their use.
Difficulty & Distance: This is largely flat with less than 100m of ascent, but some ramps up to the viaduct.

Title: Board Game Cafe - Birmingham
Summary:
An evening of board games, food, drink and probably polite company.  Join Outdoorlads for an evening at the popular board game cafe in Birmingham.
There's a small cover charge of £2.50 per person, per hour. This is paid when the leader books the table, so expect a message the day before to confirm your attendnace.  We will likely play in small groups of 5.
If the event is full, join the waiting list and I'll book a additional tables if there is enough interest.
Social events are great way to forge new friendships and talk about your experiences on OutdoorLads events, and to find out what is going on in the coming months.

Title: Highland Coast Kayak and Wild Camping Expedition 2026
Summary:
Now a regular expedition in our Scottish calendar, join us and local guide/antarctic kayak expert
Will Copestake
on a 3 day guided, and catered, kayak tour of Scotland's beautiful northwest coast!
This year we’re developing a “North West Explorer” style trip. Which is essentially a 'wherever the weather and tides make sense’ package somewhere between Gairloch and the North Coast, within easy reach of Ullapool - for example we may do Kylesku to Oldany over three days (a fab trip), or Summer Isles, or south toward Rhuba Reigh - there is a large area to go at and the weather will make the final call.
Wherever we choose, we will venture from our start point to seek caves, archways and sandy beaches. We should see seals and possibly porpoise and dolphins. We will take the days at our own pace - you do not need to be an experienced kayaker or even a strong swimmer. As part of the itinerary, we will take regular pauses on wild beaches and offer the chance to brew a hot drink on a wood fired kelly kettle stove.
In the evenings, we will camp on the beach and enjoy a catered meal with a glass of wine - kayaks give us the chance to carry a little bit of luxury into the islands.
Itinerary
Friday 14th Aug - Breakfast at your own accommodation - 10am - meet guide at launch site (tbc), who will give out boats and equipment, get paddling, camp on beach with catered meal and wine.
Saturday 15th Aug - Breakfast at camp before more paddling, lunch on beach, paddle camp on beach with catered meal and wine.
Sunday 16th Aug - Breakfast at camp, paddle, lunch, paddle, return to shore for 3pm.
There is lots of accommodation in the general area and we will coordinate over the coming weeks. The event leader will be staying at a local campsite the night before the event (details below) and you will be welcome to join, but you will be responsible for making your own arrangements. There may be opportunity to add some days on to the end of the trip to explore the area further on foot so, if you are planning to extend your trip, do share plans with the group!
How to Book:
The cost of this fantastic expedition is
£465 for guided paddling, equipment, and food for three days
. You will need to book directly with the provider by emailing
will@kayaksummerisles.com
and quoting "Outdoorlads" - The guide's website is
here
. The OutdoorLads refund policy does not apply - terms and conditions are as per the activity provider.
Please be aware
that it is the paying of the event fee (or deposit if agreed by the provider) that secures your place on the trip, rather than signing onto the trip via the OutdoorLads site. Where deposits are accepted, balance payments will be requested by the provider prior to August 2026.
If a waitlist builds up, the provider may be able to Co-opt an additional operator and boats so it may be worth signing up!

Title: FRIDAY FELLS - High Cup Nick
Summary:
Join Friday Fells for an 8.5 mile circular mountain walk (575m ascent) in the North Pennines National Landscape - a great way to start your weekend early.
This walk takes in the spectacular geological feature of High Cup Nick and Murton Pike with spectacular views over the Eden Valley.
We'll start the walk from the village of Murton.  We cross over to High Cup Gill and follow the valley its narrow head.  It's a short scramble up to the rim, which we'll follow for a while, taking in the views all the while.  Eventually, we'll cross to Murton Pike (594m), a fantastic viewpoint.  From there, it's a short descent to the village and the car.
At the end of the day, we'll go for an optional drink at the
The Stag Inn
in Dufton.
You can find the planned route
here
, though we may vary this depending on the abilities and ambitions of the group and weather conditions.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
New Members:
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event, do note that this walk is likely to require some experience. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Well-trained dogs are welcome but please note and abide by the
Countryside Code
. Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.

Title: FRIDAY FELLS - Bowscale & Bannerdale
Summary:
Join Friday Fells for an 8.5 mile circular mountain walk (725m ascent) in the Lake District's Northern Fells - a great way to start your weekend early.
This walk takes in the lower Wainwrights to the east of Blencathra and has spectacular views to the Pennines over the Eden Valley.
We'll start the walk from the village of Mungrisdale.  A short section on a quiet road will bring us to the hamlet of Bowscale. From there, we will begin our ascent, first along a track to the secretive Bowscale Tarn, from where we'll climb more steeply to Bowscale Fell (702m), our first Wainwright of the day.  From there we'll traverse across to Bannerdale Crags (683m), our second Wainwright.  A steep descent to a footbridge followed by a short climb, brings us to our third and final Wainwright of the day, Souther Fell (522m).  From there, it's a short descent to the village and the car.
At the end of the day, we'll go for an optional drink at the
Mill Inn
in Mungrisdale.
You can find the planned route
here
, though we may vary this depending on the abilities and ambitions of the group and weather conditions.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.

Title: Disused Railway Walk to Loudon Hill
Summary:
Note the change of date to July 11th
An easy linear walk from the village of Darvel along a fantastic disused railway track to the Spirit of Scotland Monument
An easy yet super scenic route from the town of Darvel, meandering along a recently upgraded disused railway track that leads to the impressive Spirit of Scotland Monument beneath Loudoun Hill. Keep an eye out for the resident Highland Cows and remnants of the area's railway past, including the striking remains of a viaduct. Return via the same route to form a 7 mile walk
From the monument why not take the opportunity to make the short but steep ascent up Loudon Hill.  On a clear day your efforts will be rewarded with incredible panoramic views over Ayrshire, Lanarkshire, the Firth of Clyde and Isle of Arran.
Regular bus services to Darvel from Kilmarnock. No public transport to Spirit of Scotland monument. Parking available on or around
Hastings Square
, Darvel (KA17 0DR) or in the
public car park on Radoldcoup Road
(KA17 0JU).
Dogs:
The walk is suitable for dogs, which must be kept under control at all times, particularly near livestock.
Photos:
All with permission gillianswalks.com

Title: Glasgow evening walk - Carnbooth AA Battery
Summary:
Glasgow evening walk - Carnbooth AA Battery
An evening walk from Queens park to Linn park and Carnbooth AA Battery.
This former World War II artillery defense site was built to guard the industrial city of Glasgow. This was one of at least 43 similar sites around the city constructed to intercept any incoming air attack from German forces on industrial sites across the banks of the River Clyde.
A list of these sites and locations can be found here:
https://www.secretscotland.org.uk/index.php/Secrets/AABatteriesClyde
The battery site originally held four gun emplacements, two magazine buildings, a command post and a gun store. Interestingly, this site was later modified during the Cold War to counter Soviet threats. It was during this period that some of the buildings were demolished and a command post, engine house, and a perimeter security fence were constructed. The bolts from the metal structures of the anti-aircraft artillery still remain on the floor of each gun base - see photo.
This is primarily an evening walk through the greener parts of the south side. The ruins at the end are an interesting bonus.
Please don't expect The Guns of Navarone !

Title: Yeavering Hell!
Summary:
A 6.5 mile very hilly walk to Northumberland’s finest Hill Fort. This route takes in two peaks with outstanding views with a slow 3 mile walk up.
Yeavering Bell is probably the leader's favourite Northumberland inland walk but only if stop for a couple of breaks along the way. On the last venture out the leader saw a couple of adders but no wild mountain goats which was a shame.
Although nowhere near as high as The Cheviot it feels like it after the effort of the ascent. The route is a challenging walk and is rewarding with a lot to see.
Image credits:
Images provided by event leader and co-leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them
Difficulty & Distance: Steep climbs first part of the walk. Rocky walk getting back.

Title: Glasgow Mural Trail
Summary:
An evening social walk around the artistic murals in Glasgow city.
These murals were intended to brighten up the gabel ends of tenements and are now a common sight around the city. They each have a story around them or commemorate a landmark event.
We won't get round every one, but will get in as many as possible over a couple of hours.
Background stories on each mural and rough locations can be found on the links below.
https://www.citycentremuraltrail.co.uk/murals/murallist
Use the link below during the walk to help you find the murals - not all of them are obvious!
https://www.citycentremuraltrail.co.uk/murals/muralmap

Title: Creating Event Pages - How not to balls it up.
Summary:
This is event is intended for new leaders or leader applicants who are due to be assessed soon. Any other leaders who feel that they need a refresher are also welcome to attend, subject to availability of places.
We will walk you through the process of creating your event page from scratch, including adding a venue and uploading your event images. We will also show you how to clone an event page and explain other important aspects of event admin such as pre-event messaging and completing the attendance register.

Title: River Tyne Route - Downstream to the Toon
Summary:
Join Steven for a pleasant easy linear valley walk hiking from a small village east and downstream to the quayside area of Newcastle Upon Tyne.
This is a much easier route, the paths are well maintained and signed and we will make a decent pace.
Along the way we will be visiting local landmarks, places of historic interest and nice venues for a swift half and a bite to eat.
Considering this route walks through the outskirts of the city this is a scenic and memorable route that largely coincides with the Hadrian's Wall Path and  River Tyne Trail
This walk is suitable for dogs, although they will be need to be on a lead in the small sections by the busy roads, a lot of the footpaths are enclosed away from farm animals. There may be a very small number of stiles to lift dogs across but as I recall they are mainly gates here.
This is an ideal way to start the weekend , there is a social later in the evening and this walk finishes in good time to check into a hotel to freshen up or return home and head back out.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/events/geordies-are-coming-toon-social-2607…
Image credits:
Images are provided by the event leader Steven and OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: 11.25 miles (18.1km) almost entirely flat

Title: River Tees evening stroll
Summary:
Join Steven and the OutdoorLads for an easy early evening wander along the riverbank visiting the pretty Georgian town of Yarm on the south bank of the Tees in the historic county of North Yorkshire.
The route takes in part of the Teesdale Way and wanders uphill to the village of Egglescliffe on the historic County Durham side.
This walk will be done at a slower relaxed pace with several stops along the way, a few points of historic note will be highlighted by the walk leader who lives locally and is very familiar with this part of the Tees Valley.
The gradients are easy and this is an ideal walk as an introduction to the group.  There are good wide reaching  views across the river and to a greater distant prospect over to the south towards the Cleveland Hills and further to the east to the Eston Hills above Middlesbrough.
So please come along to a shorter walk in pleasant surroundings along the River Tees a great way to spend a summer evening.
We are likely to gather in one of the many cafes or public houses in the area for a swift half ,tea or coffee after. If the weather is dry and warm there are outside areas and beer gardens.
Photo Credits: All photographs were taken by the walk leader Steven anf permission has been given for OutdoorLads to use them.
I
Difficulty & Distance: Easy introduction walk to the area 6.75km, 4.2 miles good paths

Title: A summer walk to Castle Eden Dene
Summary:
Join Steven for a hike in the little visited southeast corner of County Durham.
This route explores areas now managed as nature reserves and previously the land fell within the boundaries of Blackhall Colliery.
There are great views north and south along the cliff top section with only one steeper climb.
The half way point coincides with a good Public House so a longer stop here will give time for hot food.
The slow rise  from the Heritage Coast path into the magnesian limestone steep sided dene has many impressive tall trees towering above the footpaths and birdlife in abundance.
The trail passes under a high railway viaduct after leaving the coastal path this is a great place to photograph.
Although a little over 13 miles this route enables a quicker pace with a majority of good clear paths and minor roads.
Photo Credits: All photographs are taken by the leader Steven and permission has been granted for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk around 13 miles/21km. The terrain is not at all challenging and faster progress will be made.

Title: North Gloucestershire Social in Cheltenham
Summary:
An early evening, mid-week pub meet-up for members in this area. A chance for us to make closer links with other  members living nearby. An LGBTQ+ friendly venue.
Socials are an easy way to meet new and regular members and learn more about OutdoorLads before booking on your first walk, climb or hostel weekend. They're also great opportunity to find out what's coming up over the next few months with OutdoorLads in South Wales and the South West as well as nationally.
If you are coming along for the first time don't worry - the event leader will ensure that you are made welcome :-)
If you have any questions then please feel free to message the event leader. A food menue is attached if you think you may want to order food:
https://sandfordparkalehouse.co.uk/food-menu/

Title: Black Isle Coastal Walk with UK's third tallest lighthouse
Summary:
A nine mile circular walk with your OutdoorLads friends (even if you don't know them yet!)
Eight of the nine miles is coastal - hugging a narrow peninsula that juts out into the North Sea, between the Moray and Dornoch Firths. If the skies are clear, we will be rewarded with views of the hills and mountains of Caithness and Sutherland in the distance, crystal clear waters, an abundances of wildlife (seabirds, dolphins, whales (NB, the likelihood of seeing these declines the further down the list you get although whale bones are guaranteed!)), the third tallest lighthouse in the UK, some gorgeous sandy coves and rocky outcrops.
At the furthest point of our walk, with the lighthouse to our backs, we have a sea vista of some 340 degrees. We might want to dwell a while, unless there's a gale blowing!
So, bring your waterproofs and windproofs (just in case) some food, drink and enjoy OutdoorLads' company on a summer's day.
Dogs are welcome on this walk, under close control because of wildlife and livestock in fields.

Taken by Rob Mitchell and used with permission.

Title: Classic Pennine Moorland between Lancashire and Yorkshire
Summary:
A ramble of 7.5 miles (12km) with all the classic features of Pennine Moorland.
Our route follows cascading streams and moorland paths.
Among ubiquitous Victorian reservoirs there are vistas across grouse moors and outcrops of sandstone dating back 350 million years.
Curlews, Skylarks and Lapwings will  add to the magic of a moorland walk in summer.
If the weather has been inclement shortly before the walk a diversion is planned that will add approximately 2 miles to the walk.
If the weather is poor on the day, an alternative route of 6 miles will avoid the exposed moorland route.
This is a day walk on exposed moorland. Please ensure you are appropriately dressed for a day of walking in potentially very hot and sunny conditions or very wet and cold conditions.
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.

There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Difficulty & Distance: 7.5 miles on moorland tracks and paths

Title: BIG Peaky Weekender 2026 (Hostel)
Summary:
🏔️ BIG Peaky Weekender: The BIG20 Edition 🏔️
Some events are just instant classics. Born during our 15th Anniversary celebrations, the
BIG Peaky Weekender
has quickly become a legendary staple of the OutdoorLads calendar. Now, as we hit our
BIG20
, we’re heading back to the heart of the Peak District for a celebration of where we’ve been and where we’re going!
⛺ Choose Your Base Camp
We have exclusive use of this fantastic outdoor centre. Whether you’re a hostel regular or a hardcore camper, we’ve got a spot for you:
Hostel
Camping
Teepee Camping Add-on
🥾 Getting Out More
This site is perfectly located for everything we love: walking, cycling, mountain biking, climbing, and paddlesports. Since it joined our calendar five years ago, it’s become a member favorite for its "ace in every way" location.
On Saturday, our Leaders will be out in force. Expect a "Greatest Hits" selection of hikes, rides, and local adventures. On Sunday there will be a small selection of events for before we head back home. We'll list the event pages below when we have them. If you're a Leader and keen to lead something, get in touch!
🍻 The BIG20 Social: Community & Connection
While other BIG events can be quite high energy, BIG Peaky stays true to its roots as a relaxed, social homecoming.
Our legendary bar-in-a-teepee is back for the BIG20! This is about getting out more and catching up with one another. No late-night disco—just a huge, welcoming space to chat, catch up with old friends, make new ones, and maybe get a bit competitive over some board games.
🍔 Fuelling the Anniversary
Look out for meal booking pages in the weeks before the event. You can pre-book Friday and Saturday evening meals, breakfasts, and packed lunches.
Hostel attendees have the option of catering for themselves in the hostel kitchen/dining facilities. However, you must tidy up everything after yourself as if you were never there. Like any other event, we do not have cleaning staff, and we rely on and expect attendees to clean up after themselves.
✨ Be Part of the Story
From its birth at our 15th anniversary to this massive 20th celebration, the BIG Peaky Weekender is all about the people. Whether you’ve been with us for twenty years or twenty minutes, you are part of the legend.
Don’t miss out on this chapter of our BIG20 year. Grab your spot now!
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs - they are not allowed on site.
Campervans, caravans, motorhomes:
Due to the venues restrictions, we are unable to accept campervans, caravans and motorhomes.
Images:
Aerial shots kindly supplied by and used with permission from Will Allaway. Other photos taken by and used with permission from OutdoorLads Members and photographer William Hickey.
Difficulty & Distance: As much or a little as you please!

Title: Wind In The Willows River Summer 😎 Sunday Saunter
Summary:
Join us for a relaxed and sociable 10-mile circular walk through some of the most quintessentially English stretches of the Thames Valley – with one cheeky hill to earn your lunch. This is classic
“Wind in the Willows” country
. The Wind in the Willows was inspired by this very stretch of river, where Kenneth Grahame spent part of his childhood in Cookham. As we walk beside the Thames, it’s not hard to imagine Ratty and Mole “messing about in boats” along these same waters. Think lazy riverbanks, boats drifting by, and just enough charm to make you feel like you should be carrying a picnic hamper (or at least a decent snack).
The route is 10 miles, however there is the option just to do the morning section (just over 5 miles) and take the train back from Marlow Station.
The Route:
Starting at Cookham Station, we will initially walk into the older centre of the village, past the Stanley Spencer Gallery. We will then walk through the churchyard of the scenic Holy Trinity Church and then join the Thames Path, following the river for about 1.5 miles.
Later we will diverge from the Thames Path walking uphill towards Winter Hill where we are rewarded with sweeping views across the Thames Valley. We will follow the ridge for some time before climbing back down and then taking a flatter route towards Marlow. Entering Marlow, we will walk over it's historic bridge (see more details below) before getting to it's lively High Street.
At lunch we will take an extended break enabling participants to do their own thing, whether grabbing a coffee and sandwich in one of the cafes or a picnic lunch in the riverside park.
After lunch we will re-join the Thames path, which is now on the North side of the river, and walk along it for 3 miles towards Bourne End with it's marina. At the railway bridge we cross back to the South side of the river, re-trace some of our steps from the morning walk and then take a path back towards Cookham Station following the railway line across fields and later passing by a golf course.
Depending on the time that we arrive back, mindful that for those taking the train services are just hourly, there may be time for a quick drink or tea and cake at the nearby cafe (which closes at 4pm on Sundays).
The Sites:
Cookham:
is a quintessential Thames-side village, widely regarded as one of the most picturesque in England. The village has a strong artistic identity, most notably through its association with Stanley Spencer, whose work vividly depicted local life; and his legacy is preserved in the Stanley Spencer Gallery.
Winter Hill:
located just above Cookham, is characterised by its elevated position and sweeping views across the Thames Valley and the Chilterns. Much if it is protected by the National trust.
Marlow:
is a lively and attractive Georgian town that blends historic character with a strong leisure and dining scene. The town’s elegant suspension bridge, designed by William Tierney Clark, is a notable landmark and the famous Chain Bridge in Budapest, designed by the same architect, is a larger scale replica of Marlow Bridge.
Bourne End:
is a village where the Thames meets the River Wye and has a marina and railway bridge which we will pass and cross. The area also played a role in early British film production at Riverside Studios.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, the sections along the Thames Path can get busy, the lunch spot in Marlow can get very busy and there are sections walking along pavements alongside busy roads. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos:
All photos the leader's own, with permission given to OutdoorLads for use.
Difficulty & Distance: 10 miles, relaxed moderate pace, mainly flat with just one hilly section around Winter Hill, total ascent 110 meters.

Title: Northern Ireland's Causeway Coast
Summary:
We're off over the Irish Sea for four nights to explore the Causeway Coast.
Our hostel has a sea view and is in an ideal location for seeing the various attractions of the area - top of the list is the Causeway Coast itself with its hexagonal basalt columns, this being part of a fine coastal walk. Also within walking distance of the hostel is Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge, perhaps not for those with a fear of heights as it hangs 30m above the sea!
A short drive away is Ballycastle with the historic Bonamargy Friary and it's harbour, this being the port for the short sailing to Rathlin Island - an island six miles long with tranquil walking trails and views back across the causeway coast. If we go a little further east we find ourselves in the Glens of Antrim, an area of ancient woodlands and waterfalls with several beautiful walking trails.
There is lots more to see and do, but why not just come along and discover this stunning part of Northern Ireland....
Our hostel has lots of social space, free table tennis & pool and clean, comfortable dorm rooms - plus a pub 100m away. It's absolutely perfect as a base to explore the local area and this stunning coastline.
For some travel guidance, do check the information provided below the location map.

Title: Dales of the White Peak
Summary:
This walk highlights the classic dale features of the White Peak, itself part of the wider Peak District National Park.
Starting in the pretty market town of Bakewell, we head upwards onto Haddon Fields and the glorious 360 degree vistas of the White Peak including the villages of Stanton in the Peak and Youlgreave.
We head down to the historic hamlet of Alport and into our first dale - Bradford Dale. The river Bradford runs through the dale and there are signs throughout of industrial heritage. The dale itself is lush with greenery and has a small intimate feel.
Finally, we make our way up and out of the dale and head over on the Limestone Way to a very different Dale- Lathkill Dale. This large, expansive dale is popular with walkers with foreboding white cliffs and the River Lathkill running its way through.
Toward the end of our walk we head sharply towards the small village of Over Haddon before heading back down to Bakewell.
Difficulty & Distance: This walk is approximately 19km with some sharp ascents amongst the rolling landscape. Total ascent 400m

Title: Oasts and Ghosts in the Low Weald of Kent
Summary:
An introduction to the
Low Weald of Kent
, riding alongside the hop farms, oast-houses, woods and wineries that give this area its distinctive style, coupled with a climb up the Green Sands Ridge for views over the High Weald and North Downs.
We may catch the end of the bluebell season; they have put in an early appearance this year, but look stunning in the dappled shade of the Kent woodland.
This event is aimed at
experienced riders
with a reasonable fitness level who can ride at an
average pace of 14mph.
If this is faster than you would normally ride, or if you've not been in the saddle for a while, then please consider attending a Leisure Ride or an easy road ride in the first instance. This ride is
70km
, so again please ensure you are able to complete this distance comfortably.
Route Overview
We will start in the village of Marden, and head out to the Kent Weald countryside. Keep an eye out for the distinctive oast houses, many of which have been converted into homes. These round or square building were used in the drying of the hops to make beer.
After a short ride beside farmland, a hop field and ancient woodland we will pass Hush Heath Winery. The grapes here are grown on the same chalk ridges that extend to the Champagne Region of France.
We then continue east, skirting the village of Staplehurst, through the village of Frittenden and cross country to Bethersden.  Watch out for grass and grit in the centre of these narrow lanes and also be on the look out for tractors and people riding horses. There is a shop in Bethersden to get supplies if needed.
We can stop for lunch in Bethersden at The Bull Inn (
https://www.bullinnbethersden.co.uk/dining
) who do a range of baguettes and other pub snacks. Or we can continue and stop in Pluckley.
When we leave the village we head north on the slow steady incline to Pluckley. Pluckley sits on the Green Sands Ridge, that runs from Sevenoaks to the coast. This is rumoured to be the most haunted village in England, so keep an eye out for any ghosts!
The alternative lunch stop is The Black Horse in Pluckley (
https://theblackhorseatpluckley.co.uk/#menu
) also with baguettes and pub bites.
We then ride along the Green Sands Ridge, you will see to the High Weald on the south side, and to the north the North Downs. When we get to the village of Egerton, we come down from the ridge and start to wind our way towards Headcorn.
We skirt the edge of Headcorn and make our way back to finish in Marden.
Weather Conditions
If unfavourable weather is forecast, I will postpone the ride. I’ll notify participants as early as possible.
Please check messages before leaving for the event.

Title: Sheffield Bouldering - All Abilities (beginners welcome)
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at The Climbing Hanger in Sheffield.
Cards/contactless ONLY  - this venue does not accept cash
If you haven't been to The Hangar before, please click
here
to pre-register. Click on the link, scroll down, select Sheffield and follow the on-screen instructions. Completing the waiver prior to arrival will get you climbing faster.
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by the event leader / venue.
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: A Wander round Wheelton and Brindle
Summary:
Join Stephen on an 8.5 mile walk in and around Wheelton and Brindle in Central Lancashire
Highlights of the walk will be-
Walking along the Leeds and Liverpool canal
Wondering around the picturesque village of
Withnell Fold
Meandering across the fields to the village of
Brindle
Taking in the church yard and maybe a swift drink at the Cavendish Arms in Brindle
Heading to Top O' th' Lane to then walk along the edge of
Denham Hill Quarry
On a clear day, you can see as far as the Lake District
Then heading along the remains of an old canal
Picking up the start of a series of locks on the Leeds and Liverpool canal at Johnson's Hillock -
Lots of photo opportunities here
.
Having a drink at the
Top Lock
pub -
Rude not to
!
Then wandering back to the start through Wheelton village or the canal
If open , we may be able to have a brew and cake at Whin's Kitchen cafe before we finish the walk

Title: Summer Days in the Cairngorms
Summary:
Join us for three nights in the western Cairngorms
The plan for this weekend is to have a couple of big hill days in the area, there are
no low level options planned for this event
.
We have the western Cairngorm plateau that comes off the back of Braigh Riabhach with a couple of hills around the remote and little visited Glen Eidart. Also to our west is the Monadhliath, a lesser explored area but if you're bagging the munros there are four over there to be climbed. Plus it's not too far to drive to pick up other hills - Creag Meagaidh, the Laggan hills and as for Corbetts...!  Not expecting any snow to be left this summer, but the photos here were taken in June 2021.
Our base for the weekend is a Mountaineering Scotland hut at the top of Glen Feshie, simple but clean and comfortable accommodation and is a great location for getting up the hills that make the upper Speyside area. The hut has a mix of bunk beds and an alpine sleeping room, mattresses are provided but you will need to bring bedding.
Dogs:
Sorry, dogs are not permitted at this venue.

Title: BIG20: North Yorkshire Coastal Weekend
Summary:
BIG20 events are our programme to celebrate our 20th birthday year
- showcasing some of the best and most loved locations and venues we've been to in the last 20 years! We are returning again to one of our favourite hostels in the quiet and quaint village of Robin Hoods Bay, a small fishing village, surrounded by cliffs, 8km south of Whitby. The village, which consists of a maze of tiny streets, has a tradition of smuggling and there is reputed to be a network of subterranean passageways linking the houses. Situated at the end of Wainwright's Coast to Coast path and also on the Cleveland Way, the village is ideally located for coastal and moorland walks and lies within the North York Moors National Park.
Our accommodation is an independent hostel high up on the cliffs overlooking the village.
Itinerary
On Friday evening, there will be a briefing followed by the usual buffet - please bring a suitable food item.
A light breakfast will be provided on Saturday morning, following which there will be at least one walk. We may be able to offer more than one walk option, depending on leader availability. After the walk, there will be an opportunity to relax, explore the village and visit some of the excellent pubs.
On Saturday evening a meal will be provided in the hostel.
Following breakfast on Sunday morning, once we have cleaned and vacated the hostel, a shorter walk will be available for those who don't need to dash off.
Image Credits:
Provided by the hostel venue and OutdoorLads members. Used by OutdoorLads with full permission.
Dogs
Sorry this event is not suitable for dogs.

Title: Marshes & Meadows: Parkgate to Neston
Summary:
Hi all,
Join Jay as we explore another beautiful corner of the Wirral.
This relaxed, mostly flat walk is perfect if you’re new to OutdoorLads, or simply looking to meet new people and make friends in an easy-going setting.
We’ll start by heading along a scenic path towards Parkgate, with the option to grab an ice cream along the way. From there, we continue along the King Charles III England Coast Path towards Gayton, before looping back via Backwood Hall. We’ll then join the Wirral Way for a stretch, before making our way back to the cars.
To round off the day, we’ll stop at Nets Café for a well-earned cuppa and cake, just a short distance from where we finish.
Distance:
19 km (11 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.

Title: Scenic Denbighshire Hills
Summary:
Hi All,
Join me in Denbighshire's Beautiful Landscape for this walk.
Cicular Walk;
Starting and finishing in Bodfari, this delightful walk takes us partly along the famous Offa's Dyke Path, up
Penycloddiau
with it's 360 views across the
hills of Wales
.
We then make our way through Coed Llangwyfan & Penycloddiau where we turn westwards and head back towards Bodfari, descending gently down the forest path.
We finish at
The Dinorben Arms Pub

We will stop for lunch on route, hoping we get good weather for it

Title: Inverbervie and Johnshaven Circular Walk
Summary:
Circular walk along the Aberdeenshire coast with inland return
Inverbervie is the birthplace of Hercules Linton, the designer of the famous tea clipper Cutty Sark. We head down to the beach and trace the coastline south to the fishing villages of Gourdon and Johnshaven. We then head slightly inland to Mill of Benholm and return to Inverbervie by an old coaching route with good views of the sea from the highest point of the walk at Gourdon Hill (136 metres). The route is by paths, old railway line and sections of beach. May be muddy in places.
Transport
Car parking (free) is available in the square and the Stagecoach X7 Coastrider bus service between Dundee and Aberdeen stops at the square.

Title: Dunnottar Castle and Woods Circular Walk
Summary:
6 mile circular walk to Dunnottar Castle and nearby Dunnottar Woods
Our walk starts in the market square in Stonehaven and we head towards the boardwalk along the shore before starting a short but steep climb uphill providing lovely views looking down to Stonehaven Harbour. In a short while we reach the War Memorial before continuing on the path to historic Dunnottar Castle. The plan is to visit the castle (optional) before continuing our walk slightly inland towards Dunnottar Woods and heading through the woods and back down to Stonehaven where there are ample opportunities to have fish and chips or ice cream  - or both!
Dunnottar Castle Prices
: £13.00 or £11.50 concession.
The castle is a ruined medieval fortress but with some surviving buildings. The Honours of Scotland (Scottish Crown Jewels of crown, sword and sceptre) were hidden in the castle to prevent Oliver Cromwell's invading army getting hold of them. The castle and surrounds affords some great photo opportunities.
Transport
There is a car park in Market Square (charge) but there are plenty of side streets nearby with free parking. The railway station is a 15 minute walk to Market Square. The X7 Stagecoach Coastrider bus service between Dundee and Aberdeen stops in Stonehaven.

Title: Ledsham and Fairburn Ings Evening Walk
Summary:
This easy 8km circular summer evening walk will begin in the pretty Yorkshire village of Ledsham, just 10 miles from Leeds City centre. Good paths and tracks take us from the village through fields and woods towards Fairburn Ings RSPB Nature Reserve. This former coal mine is now a centre for biodiversity including woodland, pools, grassland and marshland. Taking the path around the reserve we may see a variety of wildlife, including a Kingfisher. Our path home takes us back across fields and woodlands and into Ledsham. Hopefully a sunny summer evening will allow us to sit and have a drink in the beer garden at the Chequers Inn pub before we head home.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Image credits: All images from Geograph.org.uk and used with Creative Commons Licence: Fairburn Ings by Derek Dye; Cheques Inn by Gordon Hatton; Ledsham Walk by Ian S.
Difficulty & Distance: 5 miles/8km 2.5 hours

Title: Settle to Stainforth ft. caves and waterfalls…
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a 9-mile circular walk from Settle to Stainforth in North Yorkshire. We will start our journey through the quaint market town centre before an uphill ascent via the Dales Highway, with spectacular views of the surrounding countryside and town below.
Our route will then take us through Attermire Scar; here we will pass the larger Victoria Cave and smaller Jubilee Cave, carved into the limestone cliff. Victoria cave was discovered in 1837 during the reign of Queen Victoria, hence the name. It is considered an important archaeological site due to the discovery of animal bones up to 130,000 years old! There was also the discovery of 11,000-year-old tools and artefacts, providing the first evidence of human settlement in the area.
On our descent into Stainforth we will pass Catrigg Force, a waterfall cascading over a deep gorge. The beautiful village of Stainforth awaits; we will stop for lunch here after crossing some stepping stones over Stainforth Beck. After lunch, we will pass another waterfall, Stainforth Force, before taking a gentle stroll along the bank of the River Ribble leading us all the way back to Settle. There will be an opportunity to stop for a drink at one of the local establishments in Settle at the end of the walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
All photos taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is approximately 9-miles long through mixed terrain including a couple of steep uphill climbs. It can be quite uneven and rocky underfoot in places.

Title: Mynydd Mawr - An easier Eryri Panorama
Summary:
Mynydd Mawr (Welsh for 'big mountain') overlooks Llyn Cwellyn and is at the edge of the Moel Hebog group. It's actually by no means one of the biggest mountains but because it stands out it looks to be that way. On its western flank are the remains of ancient settlements and field systems. Set apart from the main mountain ranges it affords fantastic views without the crowds. The summit offers extensive views, on a clear day the views extend to the Llŷn Peninsula and Caernarfon Bay, Anglesey, Moel Eilio, Moel Hebog, Yr Wyddfa and beyond.
This event is ideal for those looking for a bit more of a challenge from a day walk, but who don't feel up to a whole day of full on mountain hiking. As mountains go, it's not a difficult ascent and as such is offered as a hill walk, but be in no doubt it is an uphill route and reasonable fitness is needed.
After leaving the meeting point we soon begin the steady ascent, initially through a forest, then on to open moorland, where the views keep on expanding. It's a well trodden path in the main and when we level out at the summit there's a 360-degree panorama. We will be going back down the same way, but as you'll be facing the other direction everything will look better.
Photos:
AndyM, event leader - taken from a previous trip, these give you a sense of the landscape you'll see.
Bad Weather
This event will be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain, high winds, lightning or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case.
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note the following:
We may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead.
Not everyone likes dogs so please ensure your furry pal doesn't worry other members.
There may be obstacles to carry your dog across, and your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group.

Title: Foxton Locks and the Grand Union Canal
Summary:
Foxton Locks
is a Grade II listed site - home to the UK's longest and steepest staircase of ten locks. In 1810, an Act of Parliament authorised a new section of canal to be built from the Leicestershire and Northamptonshire Canal at Foxton to the Grand Junction Canal at Buckby. For the first time, boats and cargos could travel from the River Thames at Brentford all the way through to the East Midlands, including the coalfields of Nottinghamshire and Derbyshire.
However, a bottleneck of boats trying to pass through so many locks prompted the building of the
Foxton Inclined Plane
– a steam-powered mechanical lift system – alongside. The inclined plane had a journey time of just 12 minutes for two boats up and two down, compared with over an hour through the lock system. But dwindling canal traffic in the face of competition from the railways meant the Inclined Plane closed in 1911. Today, you can see the remains of this scheduled Ancient Monument and learn about its history in the Foxton Locks Museum.
Our walk takes us across the fields from Foxton to the village of
Laughton
and an opportunity to enjoy the tranquility of the beautiful rolling hills of South Leicestershire.
From Laughton, we head across to
Saddington
- a small hilltop village which overlooks the Saddington Reservoir, constructed in 1802 to feed the Grand Union Canal. We'll stop for lunch in the village churchyard which just happens to be right beside the
Queen's Head
pub for anyone who's in need of a pick-me-up!
Next, we head down towards the
Saddington Reservoir
before veering off to join the
Grand Union Canal
and a 4 mile stroll along the meandering tow path, passing Debdale Wharf Marina before arriving back at Foxton Locks.
With two pubs, two cafés and a canal museum, there'll be plenty of scope for further refreshments at Foxton Locks and to explore the bustling 34 acre site at your own pace.
Dogs are welcome:
Foxton Locks is a very popular visitor attraction – dogs will need to be kept on a lead. Our walk includes some short stretches on country roads and village streets. We may encounter sheep, lambs, cows or horses and there are a few fields where landowner signs request dogs on a lead.
Waiting List:

OutdoorLads

Title: Cambridge, Grantchester and Byron's Pool
Summary:
Join Alex for this ten-mile walk around colleges, parks and meadows in Cambridge and Granchester.
We will begin at Cambridge station and head into the city, crossing the wide open green of Parker's Piece, the historic field where the rules of football were codified in 1863. From there we will continue north through the streets of Cambridge until we reach the River Cam. We will follow the riverbank past the parklands of Midsummer Common and Jesus Green as the Cam curves through the city.
Turning south we will pass several constituent colleges of Cambridge University, including St John's, Trinity and the grandest of them all, King's. Here we will cross the river and turn south along the Backs where we will have views of King's College Chapel and the Mathematical Bridge, both symbols of the city, as well as punters gliding along the water with varying degrees of success.
Continuing southwards, we will leave the city centre and head through the suburb of Newnham, still following the River Cam as it leads us out toward open countryside. We will continue upstream, crossing Granchester Meadows and passing through the Orchard Tea Garden, once frequented by famous writers such as Rupert Brooke, EM Forster and Virginia Woolf. Our route will then take us through Grantchester itself, past the picturesque parish church and out of the village towards Byron's Pool, a section of the river named after the romantic poet who used to swim here.
Leaving Grantchester we will head east across Trumpington Meadows and the turn north where our route will join the tree-lined path beside Vicar's Brook, a small chalk stream. We will follow this wooded nature reserve back towards the city centre and our starting point back at the station.
Don't waste that space!
Summer always means lots of new and existing members excited to be getting back out more. Please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.

Title: Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Summary:
This is the first of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 2 (Saturday 27th June) here:
Lochinver 2: Quinaig
Day 3 (Sunday 28th June) here:
Lochinver 3: Canisp
Watching the sunset is, of course, subject to weather conditions but we will set off to hike one of the many coastal rises in the Lochinver area in time to wait on the summit as the sun sinks in the west, over the sea. Behind us will see the sunset colours, with luck, change the look of one of Scotland's most iconic mountains, Suilven. After we've have of fill of evening glory we'll return to Lochinver where we can have a drink or two before ending our day.
Accommodation Options:
An Cala Bunkhouse
Culag Hotel

Title: Lochinver 2: Quinag (Three Corbetts)
Summary:
This is the second of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 1 (Friday 26th June) here:
Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Day 3 (Sunday 28th June) here:
Lochinver 3: Canisp
This is a stunning and quite complex mountain or three separate Corbetts to conquer. Some argue that this is one of the finest mountain walks in the Highlands. It has pointy peaks, open and airy ridges and amazing views (weather permitting!).
It's an 8.5 hour hike, with a total height gain of 1130m, over 14km / 8.75miles. It's a circular walk with spurs!. The Corbetts' names are Spidean Coininch, Sail Gorm and Sail Garbh.
Corbetts are a classification of mountain to heights of between 2500 and 2999 feet.

Title: Lochinver 3: Canisp (Corbett)
Summary:
This is the third of three, consecutive, separate day events from Lochinver in the NW Highlands. Book one, two or all three events as you wish! Book your own accommodation if you wish to stay overnight, some suggestions below.
Day 1 (Friday 26th June) here:
Lochinver 1: Watch the Summer Sunset over the Sea
Day 2 (Saturday 27th June) here:
Lochinver 2: Quinaig
A challenging, out-and-back hike to the summit of Canisp. It's only 12km or 7.5 miles but it's across often pathless, boggy and stony ground making the going very slow at times. We will have 710 metres of ascent so we will be rewarded, with a bit of luck, with some very dramatic views from the summit. Iconic mountains such as Suilven and Stac Pollaidh dominate the sky-line, but the vastness of the landscape of Assynt can take your breath away. There are lots of mountains to see in the distance but unlike much of the Highlands where they're squashed in together, almost holding each other up, these rise individually and dramatically from a boggy flat landscape which is one of the richest carbon deposits in Europe.
Canisp is a Corbett, which is a classification of mountain between 2,500 and 2,999 feet. The summit is 847m high (2779 feet).
IMPORTANT -
DO NOT USE THE POSTCODE FOR SATNAV PURPOSES.
Follow the text directions.

Title: Birmingham Canals and Edgbaston Resevoir
Summary:
Easy 8 mile circular walk from central Birmingham onto the Birmingham canal old line at Brindley Place before making our way to Edgbaston Reservoir. Edgbaston Reservoir, originally known as Rotton Park Reservoir and referred to in some early maps as Rock Pool Reservoir is a canal feeder reservoir maintained by the Canal and River Trust. This will be our lunch stop before following a hidden disused railway line to Harborne before joining the Worcester and Birmingham canal back to the city via the Mail Box.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk.
There will be opportunity for a coffee or pint afterwards.

Title: Bridgnorth Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Samuel for a 10 miles/16kms walk around Bridgnorth in Shropshire.
We’ll begin in the charming “High Town” before making our way down to the river in “Low Town,” following the scenic riverside path. From there, we’ll meander along bridle paths, across fields, and through hidden woodland. The route takes us under the Severn Valley Railway line via a tunnel, then beneath the Oldbury Viaduct, passing Daniels Mill. Finally, we’ll return to town along the river and climb back up to “High Town” via the steep steps at Castle Hill.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on the walk but may need to be on a lead when passing through grazing land with livestock and horses.
